(二十二社, *Nijūni-sha*) of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") is one ranking system for [Shinto shrines](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine"). The system was established during the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") and formed part of the government's systematization of Shinto during the emergence of a general anti-Chinese sentiment and the suppression of the [Taoist](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") religion.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It involved the establishment of the shrines as important centers of public life in Japan. It played a role in official imperial ceremonies such as the *Practice of Chinkon*. An extensive body of literature also emerged containing information about each shrine, including the shrine's origin, priestly dress, divine treatises, the system of shrine removal, subordinate shrines, and annual cycle of rituals, among others.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Shinto "Template:Shinto") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Shinto "Template talk:Shinto") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Shinto "Special:EditPage/Template:Shinto") |

The **Twenty-Two Shrines** (二十二社, *Nijūni-sha*) of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") is one ranking system for [Shinto shrines](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine"). The system was established during the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") and formed part of the government's systematization of Shinto during the emergence of a general anti-Chinese sentiment and the suppression of the [Taoist](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") religion.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It involved the establishment of the shrines as important centers of public life in Japan. It played a role in official imperial ceremonies such as the *Practice of Chinkon*. An extensive body of literature also emerged containing information about each shrine, including the shrine's origin, priestly dress, divine treatises, the system of shrine removal, subordinate shrines, and annual cycle of rituals, among others.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

By the year 806, 4,870 households were assigned to Shinto shrines while the government provided a national endowment for their upkeep.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) These shrines also received special offerings from the [Imperial Court](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto"). As time progressed, this offering to the shrines was amended[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) so that Imperial envoys were only sent to the powerful shrines in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"), which was the capital of Japan at the time.[[2]](#cite_note-2) This amendment initially identified fourteen shrines but it was increased to twenty-two in 1081. There are historians who explained that the majority on the list involved those with central lineages supporting the imperial house, sites of cults that gained popular significance, and shrines in locations with the presence of [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") institutions.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twenty-Two_Shrines&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Establishment")]

Under the [Ritsuryō](/wiki/Ritsury%C5%8D "Ritsuryō") law system, the shrines that the Imperial Court would present offerings to for rites such as the *ki'nensai* (祈年祭), a service to pray for a good harvest, were mostly decided by the *[Engishiki](/wiki/Engishiki "Engishiki") Jinmyōchō* (延喜式神名帳, *Engishiki Shrine Name Book*), but once the Ritsuryō system began to deteriorate, the offerings were only given to a select few shrines.

In 965, [Emperor Murakami](/wiki/Emperor_Murakami "Emperor Murakami") ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian *kami* of Japan. These *heihaku* were presented to 16 shrines: 1. Ise; 2. Iwashimizu; 3. Kamo; 4. Matsunoo; 5. Hirano; 6. Inari; 7. Kasuga; 8. Oharano; 9. Miwa; 10: Isonokami; 11. Ōyamato; 12. Hirose; 13. Tatsuta; 14. Sumiyoshi; 15. Nibu and 16. Kibune.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 991, [Emperor Ichijō](/wiki/Emperor_Ichij%C5%8D "Emperor Ichijō") added three more shrines to Murakami's list—17. Yoshida; 18. Hirota; and 19. Kitano; and two more were added three years later in 994;—20. Umenomiya; and 21. Gion.[[5]](#cite_note-pf118-5)

In 1039, [Emperor Go-Suzaku](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Suzaku "Emperor Go-Suzaku") ordered that one more shrine be added to this list, 22. Hie, and this unique number of Imperial-designated shrines has not been altered since that time.[[5]](#cite_note-pf118-5)

Near the end of the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period"), there was a movement to add [Itsukushima Shrine](/wiki/Itsukushima_Shrine "Itsukushima Shrine") to the list, but it did not happen. However, until the end of the [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period"), the Imperial Court made offerings to it, and in the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), offerings were again made after disasters occurred.

## List of shrines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twenty-Two_Shrines&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: List of shrines")]

When the Nijūni-sha are considered as a grouped set, they are conventionally presented in order of rank, not in terms of the chronological sequence in which they were designated.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The three rank ranked groupings originally derived from a complex array of Heian geopolitical relationships.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

| Gion-sha (祇園社) | [Yasaka Jinja](/wiki/Yasaka_Shrine "Yasaka Shrine") (八坂神社) | [Higashiyama-ku](/wiki/Higashiyama-ku,_Kyoto "Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto"), Kyoto |
| Kitano Jinja (北野神社) | [Kitano Tenman-gū](/wiki/Kitano_Tenman-g%C5%AB "Kitano Tenman-gū") (北野天満宮) | [Kamigyō-ku](/wiki/Kamigy%C5%8D-ku,_Kyoto "Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto"), Kyoto |
| Niukawakami Jinja (丹生川上神社) | \*[Niukawakami Jinja](/wiki/Niukawakami_Shrine "Niukawakami Shrine") Shimosha (lower shrine) Niukawakami Jinja Nakasha (middle shrine) \*Niukawakami Jinja Kamisha (upper shrine) | [Shimoichi](/wiki/Shimoichi,_Nara "Shimoichi, Nara"), Nara [Higashiyoshino](/wiki/Higashiyoshino,_Nara "Higashiyoshino, Nara"), Nara [Kawakami](/wiki/Kawakami,_Nara "Kawakami, Nara"), Nara |
| Kibune Jinja (貴船神社) | [Kibune Jinja](/wiki/Kibune_Shrine "Kibune Shrine") (also Kifune Jinja) | Sakyō-ku, Kyoto |

* **Note:** At the time when the Nijunisha were chosen, the current **Niukawakami Nakasha** was the only Niukawakami Shrine. It became the middle shrine (*nakasha*) only after the shrine in [Shimoichi](/wiki/Shimoichi "Shimoichi") and [Kawakami](/wiki/Kawakami,_Nara "Kawakami, Nara") were united with it.
